key_difference: Depression is not a formal veterinary diagnosis -- veterinarians treat it as a behavioural/emotional change reached only as a diagnosis of exclusion after ruling out medical causes, and generally manage it with enrichment rather than antidepressant pharmacotherapy.
source_name: Texas A&M College of Veterinary Medicine, Pet Talk
note: Confirms the parallel audit's calibration; an AAHA Trends article stating 'there is no official depression diagnosis for dogs and cats' appeared in search results but the page itself returned HTTP 403 so I cite only the source I could read.

vet_counterpart_name: Canine chronic inflammatory enteropathy (CIE); histiocytic ulcerative (granulomatous) colitis of Boxers and French Bulldogs
key_difference: Human UC is an idiopathic immune-mediated colitis, whereas the same-named canine disease (histiocytic ulcerative colitis) is caused by invasive intramucosal E. coli and remits on enrofloxacin, and general canine colitis is classified as treatment-defined chronic enteropathy rather than UC.
source_name: J Vet Intern Med 2009, 'Remission of histiocytic ulcerative colitis in Boxer dogs correlates with eradication of invasive intramucosal Escherichia coli'
note: Confirms the parallel audit's calibration; Dandrieux 2016 (J Small Anim Pract, https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/jsap.12588) argues 'chronic enteropathy' is the correct canine term because treatment and outcome differ from human IBD -- I could not verify a PMID for that paper so none is cited.

vet_counterpart_name: Canine acute lymphoblastic leukemia / lymphoblastic lymphoma
key_difference: Canine ALL is almost always T-cell in origin, arises in middle-aged/older dogs (median 7-8 years) and is near-uniformly fatal (median survival 25-50 days), whereas human ALL is predominantly B-precursor, chiefly pediatric, and largely curable.
source_name: Avery AC, 'The Genetic and Molecular Basis for Canine Models of Human Leukemia and Lymphoma', Front Oncol 2020;10:23
note: Real precursor-lymphoid neoplasm in dogs, but lineage, age structure and natural history all diverge from human ALL; further doubt - up to ~50% of canine acute leukemias called lymphoid on morphology are reclassified as myeloid on immunophenotyping, so the 11 pet labels on this node may be partly mis-assigned. Feline ALL not well characterized in the sources I saw; occurs_in set to 'dog' for that reason.

vet_counterpart_name: Congestive heart failure secondary to myxomatous mitral valve disease or DCM (dogs) / hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (cats)
key_difference: The CHF syndrome itself is real in both species, but the etiologic substrate is fundamentally different: dogs fail from degenerative mitral valve disease or DCM and cats from HCM, whereas human HF is predominantly ischemic/hypertensive, and coronary artery disease and myocardial infarction are rare in dogs.
source_name: Merck Vet Manual
note: Merck explicitly frames HF as 'not a specific disease or diagnosis. It is a syndrome' — a syndrome-level MeSH node whose upstream biology diverges by species, so sim=1.0 overstates a genuinely useful but non-identical bridge.

vet_counterpart_name: Canine/feline mammary gland tumours
key_difference: Roughly half of canine mammary tumours are benign and mixed (myoepithelial-containing) tumours are the single most common canine type despite being rare in the human breast, while feline mammary tumours run ~80-90% malignant and highly metastatic - so the subtype and behaviour distribution differs sharply from human breast cancer in both directions.
source_name: Razavirad et al., 'Canine Mammary Tumors as a Potential Model for Human Breast Cancer in Comparative Oncology', Vet Med Int 2024
note: Genuine and clinically/molecularly similar disease, but dog and cat sit on opposite sides of human breast cancer's malignancy spectrum, so one 1.0 edge for a species-agnostic 'pet' node overstates both; feline 80-90% malignant figure seen in search results (Merck Vet Manual / Cornell Feline Health Center), not fetched directly.

vet_counterpart_name: Erosive immune-mediated polyarthritis (canine rheumatoid arthritis; feline rheumatoid-like arthritis)
key_difference: Canine/feline RA is a RARE erosive subset of immune-mediated polyarthritis - non-erosive IMPA is far more common and is the disease usually being treated - and rheumatoid factor performs poorly as a marker, unlike seropositive human RA where RF is part of the 2010 classification criteria.
source_name: MSD/Merck Vet Manual, Immune-Mediated Polyarthritis in Dogs and Cats
note: Independently confirms the calibration example; MSD classes RA under erosive IMPA in dogs and 'feline rheumatoid-like arthritis' in cats. Honest flag: sources conflict on rheumatoid factor - some report RF uncommon and low-titre in canine RA, another reports RF >1:8 in 72.2% of definite canine RA, so I state only that RF has limited diagnostic value, which both agree on.

vet_counterpart_name: Primary open-angle glaucoma (breed-restricted, ADAMTS10/ADAMTS17); most canine primary glaucoma is goniodysgenesis/angle-closure
key_difference: Canine POAG is a rare early-onset monogenic recessive disease of a few breeds (Beagle and Norwegian Elkhound via ADAMTS10, Basset Hound via ADAMTS17) whereas human POAG is common, adult-onset and polygenic, and most canine primary glaucoma involves progressive iridocorneal angle closure rather than an open angle.
source_name: MSD Vet Manual, 'Glaucoma in Animals'
note: POAG genuinely occurs in dogs (and in Siamese cats) and Beagles are an established model of human POAG, but it is the minority form of canine primary glaucoma and feline glaucoma is predominantly secondary to uveitis/neoplasia.

vet_counterpart_name: Canine invasive urothelial carcinoma (transitional cell carcinoma)
key_difference: Canine urothelial carcinoma is >80% high-grade muscle-invasive disease centered on the bladder trigone and carries an activating BRAF V595E mutation in ~85% of cases, whereas low-grade superficial (non-muscle-invasive) tumors predominate in humans.
source_name: Front Oncol 2022, naturally-occurring canine model of high grade urothelial carcinoma
note: Well-accepted comparative model, but specifically for high-grade invasive disease; I did not verify BRAF V600E frequency in human bladder cancer, so the mutation contrast is stated only for the canine side.

vet_counterpart_name: Juvenile myoclonic epilepsy of Rhodesian Ridgebacks (DIRAS1)
key_difference: Canine JME is a breed-restricted monogenic autosomal-recessive disorder caused by a 4-bp DIRAS1 deletion, whereas human JME shows complex/heterogeneous inheritance and DIRAS1 remains only a candidate gene never confirmed as a cause in human patients.
source_name: PNAS 2017, 'Generalized myoclonic epilepsy with photosensitivity in juvenile dogs caused by a defective DIRAS family GTPase 1'
note: Genuine clinical parallels (photosensitivity, myoclonic jerks, fronto-central spike-and-wave EEG), but the canine disease exists in one breed via a gene with no confirmed human JME role, so a 1.0 gene-level bridge is unsupported.

vet_counterpart_name: Systemic mastocytosis / disseminated visceral mast cell tumor with mastocythemia
key_difference: In dogs and cats circulating neoplastic mast cells almost always seed from a visceral or cutaneous mast cell tumor (systemic mastocytosis) rather than arising as a primary bone-marrow leukemia - true mast cell leukemia is explicitly 'rarely observed in dogs' - and the driver is a KIT exon 11 juxtamembrane ITD, not the human D816V kinase-domain mutation.
source_name: Vet Clin Pathol / J Vet Diagn Invest case report + review of diagnostic criteria, Greyhound systemic mastocytosis (PMID 33164718)
note: DRUG-REPURPOSING RED FLAG: the KIT lesion differs by domain and has OPPOSITE TKI sensitivity - human D816V mastocytosis is imatinib-resistant, while canine exon 11 ITD MCT is imatinib/toceranib-sensitive - so a sim=1.0 bridge would transfer exactly the wrong drug signal. Also, veterinary diagnosis 'must rely primarily on fulfillment of the WHO major criterion' because the minor criteria (KIT codon 816, CD25, tryptase) are not attainable in dogs/cats. Feline splenic MCT is common (15-26% of feline splenic neoplasms) but is disseminated MCT, not MCL.

vet_counterpart_name: Gastroduodenal (gastrointestinal) ulceration in small animals
key_difference: Human peptic ulcer is dominated by Helicobacter pylori plus NSAIDs, whereas canine/feline gastroduodenal ulcers are attributed to NSAIDs/glucocorticoids, neoplasia and hepatic disease, and H. pylori is not a recognized cause in dogs or cats.
source_name: MSD Vet Manual, 'Gastrointestinal Ulcers in Small Animals'
note: Ulcer lesion and acid-suppression/mucosal-protection treatment transfer well, but the H. pylori eradication arm of human peptic ulcer pharmacology has no canine/feline correlate -- dogs and cats carry non-pylori Helicobacter of unclear causal role.

vet_counterpart_name: Pemphigus foliaceus (canine/feline)
key_difference: The major autoantigen in canine pemphigus foliaceus is desmocollin-1 (DSG1 detected in only ~6% of sera), whereas human pemphigus targets desmoglein-1/desmoglein-3, and the dominant subtype is inverted (foliaceus predominates in dogs/cats, vulgaris in humans).
source_name: Vet Immunol Immunopathol (canine DSC1 as major PF autoantigen)
note: Real autoimmune blistering disease of the same family, but a different desmosomal target protein means drug/target inference across the bridge is not safe at 1.0.

pet_disease_name: Squamous Cell Carcinoma of Head and Neck
human_disease_name: Squamous Cell Carcinoma of Head and Neck
vet_counterpart_name: Canine and feline oral squamous cell carcinoma
key_difference: Canine/feline oral SCC has no true HPV-driven counterpart (it models only HPV-negative HNSCC, while ~25% of human HNSCC is high-risk HPV-associated) and shows less frequent TP53 mutation than human HNSCC.
source_name: 'Canine Spontaneous Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinomas Represent Their Human Counterparts at the Molecular Level'
note: The closest bridge in this batch after IMHA -- canine tumors recapitulate human HNSCC CNA landscape and cell-cycle/PI3K-AKT alterations -- but the HPV-positive human subtype has no animal counterpart, so 1.0 overstates it.

vet_counterpart_name: Secondary systemic hypertension (most often chronic kidney disease-, hyperthyroidism- or hyperadrenocorticism-associated)
key_difference: In dogs and cats hypertension is predominantly SECONDARY to an identifiable underlying disease, with idiopathic hypertension accounting for only ~13-20% of feline cases, whereas human hypertension is predominantly primary/essential.
source_name: ACVIM consensus statement 2018 (Acierno et al., J Vet Intern Med 32:1803-1822)
note: Same end-organ pathophysiology, but the etiologic structure is inverted vs humans; the ACVIM panel deliberately says 'idiopathic' rather than 'essential' for animals.

vet_counterpart_name: Canine cutaneous lupus erythematosus (facial/generalized discoid LE, vesicular CLE, exfoliative CLE, mucocutaneous LE)
key_difference: Canine CLE variants only partly map onto the human Gilliam-Sontheimer scheme -- some appear unique to the dog -- and canine facial discoid LE essentially never progresses to SLE (no progression in 104 dogs across the four largest case series), unlike human DLE.
source_name: BMC Vet Res 2018, 'Cutaneous lupus erythematosus in dogs: a comprehensive review'
note: A real and well-characterized canine disease (vesicular CLE is an accepted equivalent of human subacute cutaneous LE), so the edge is defensible but sim=1.0 overstates it; the review does not address cats, so I cannot confirm feline CLE and set occurs_in to dog only.

vet_counterpart_name: Canine separation distress disorder / noise phobia / generalized anxiety
key_difference: Canine anxiety is dominated by separation distress and noise phobia, diagnosed purely from observed behavior with no self-reported cognitive component, and human subtypes such as panic disorder and social anxiety disorder have no established canine counterpart.
source_name: MSD Vet Manual, 'Behavioral Problems of Dogs'
note: Unlike the 'Depression' calibration case this IS a genuine veterinary diagnosis with FDA-approved drugs (clomipramine/Clomicalm, fluoxetine/Reconcile, dexmedetomidine/Sileo), so the edge is defensible -- only the 1.0 is wrong; feline anxiety is recognized but was not verified here.

vet_counterpart_name: Canine sinonasal aspergillosis; feline sinonasal/sino-orbital aspergillosis; disseminated aspergillosis (A. terreus) of German Shepherd Dogs
key_difference: Dogs and cats mainly develop fungal rhinosinusitis as immunocompetent hosts (dolichocephalic dogs, A. fumigatus; cats, A. felis), whereas human aspergillosis is dominated by invasive pulmonary disease in immunocompromised patients.
source_name: MSD Vet Manual, 'Aspergillosis in Animals'
note: Same pathogen genus and overlapping antifungal therapy, but the disease form and host immune context differ, so the human node's immunocompromised-host biology should not flow through at 1.0.

vet_counterpart_name: Phaeohyphomycosis (dematiaceous/black fungal infection)
key_difference: True chromoblastomycosis (muriform/sclerotic bodies from traumatic inoculation) is essentially absent in dogs/cats and exists only as isolated case reports; the recognized veterinary dematiaceous mycosis is phaeohyphomycosis, defined by pigmented septate hyphae and typically affecting pinnae/digits/nasal planum in cats or immunosuppressed (cyclosporine-treated) dogs.
source_name: J Feline Med Surg 2015 case report; MSD Vet Manual (Phaeohyphomycosis in Animals)
note: The single confirmed feline case I verified was a Cladophialophora carrionii peritoneal exudate in an FIV+ cat -- atypical for chromoblastomycosis; the MSD Vet Manual page I fetched has no chromoblastomycosis entry at all, only phaeohyphomycosis. A canine case report exists in a Chilean journal but the PDF returned HTTP 403 so I did not verify it and do not cite it.

vet_counterpart_name: Canine chronic hepatitis (idiopathic / presumed immune-mediated)
key_difference: Canine AIH is not an established, criteria-defined diagnosis: dogs lack the type-2 markers (anti-LKM1, anti-LC1), and 'piecemeal necrosis and intense portal track inflammation are the pathological hallmarks of hAIH but such histological features are not frequently seen in canine livers with cAIH'.
source_name: Auto Immun Highlights 2012, canine autoimmune hepatitis autoantibody review (PMID 26000131)
note: A canine entity is claimed (ANA/SMA and anti-liver-membrane antibodies reported) but the same paper calls cAIH 'an entity that is poorly understood' with autoantibody specificities 'not thoroughly studied'; the ACVIM 2019 consensus (PMID 30844094) uses 'chronic hepatitis', treats immune-mediated CH as a presumptive diagnosis of exclusion requiring steroid response, and a large share of canine CH is copper-associated rather than autoimmune. Bridge defensible, sim=1.0 not.

vet_counterpart_name: Feline diabetes mellitus (type 2-like); canine diabetes is a separate insulin-deficient, type 1-like disease
key_difference: Type 2 diabetes is genuinely feline-only among pets - cats show insulin resistance, obesity and islet amyloidosis with IAPP differing from human by only 2 residues, whereas DOGS get an insulin-deficient type 1-like disease entirely lacking islet amyloid - and feline disease uniquely remits once glucose toxicity is reversed.
source_name: 'Comparative Pharmacological and Pharmaceutical Perspectives on Antidiabetic Therapies in Humans, Dogs, and Cats' (PMC12472474); also Henson & O'Brien, ILAR J 2006;47(3):234, 'Feline Models of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus'
note: For CATS alone this is close to EQUIVALENT and is a well-accepted model of human T2DM; I downgrade to ANALOGOUS only because a species-agnostic 'pet' node inherits dogs, in which T2DM effectively does not occur, plus frequent diabetic remission has no human T2DM analogue.

key_difference: Subtype distribution is inverted -- clear cell RCC is ~75% of human renal carcinomas but only 9% of canine cases (papillary at 21% predominates in dogs, tubular in cats), so the VHL/HIF clear-cell biology that most human RCC drugs target is not the canine norm.
source_name: Vet Pathol 2015, 'Prognostic significance of histologic features in canine renal cell carcinomas: 70 nephrectomies'
note: RCC is real and is the most common kidney cancer in dogs, and the authors argue orthologs of the human subtypes occur in dogs, but the dominant-subtype mismatch makes sim=1.0 overstated for repurposing purposes.

vet_counterpart_name: Canine primary pulmonary carcinoma (mainly pulmonary adenocarcinoma)
key_difference: No tobacco etiology is recognized in dogs and the driver landscape is inverted -- HER2 mutations in ~38% of canine pulmonary adenocarcinomas vs ~3% in humans, EGFR mutations rare in dogs vs ~45% in human never-smoker lung cancer, with ALK/ROS/RET rarely documented in dogs.
source_name: Front Vet Sci, 'Canine lung carcinoma - A descriptive review'
note: Histologically resembles human never-smoker NSCLC, but the inverted EGFR/HER2 driver frequencies make a 1.0 bridge actively misleading for targeted-therapy inference; feline primary lung tumors exist but were not verified in this audit.

vet_counterpart_name: Feline hyperthyroidism (functional thyroid adenoma / adenomatous hyperplasia); in dogs, hyperthyroidism secondary to thyroid carcinoma
key_difference: Feline hyperthyroidism is a non-autoimmune autonomous nodular disease (no circulating thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins) that models human toxic nodular goiter rather than Graves' disease, which causes most human hyperthyroidism; in dogs it is rare and almost always caused by thyroid carcinoma.
source_name: MSD Vet Manual, 'Hyperthyroidism in Animals'
note: The thyrotoxic syndrome and antithyroid drug therapy (methimazole, radioiodine) genuinely overlap, but the autoimmune mechanism dominating the human node does not transfer, and dog vs cat etiologies are themselves different diseases.

vet_counterpart_name: Canine hepatocellular carcinoma (massive/solitary form)
key_difference: Canine HCC lacks the chronic hepatitis B/C and cirrhosis background that drives the majority of human HCC (HBV alone = 55% of cases globally), and presents as a solitary resectable massive mass in >50% of dogs rather than multifocal disease on a globally cirrhotic liver.
source_name: Vet Sci 2022, Comparative Oncology: Management of Hepatic Neoplasia in Humans and Dogs
note: Real canine tumor with reported molecular similarity to human HCC, but the etiologic and staging context differs enough that antiviral/cirrhosis-driven human HCC drug associations would not transfer cleanly.

vet_counterpart_name: Primary erythrocytosis (polycythemia vera)
key_difference: Human PV is defined molecularly by JAK2 V617F in ~95% of cases, whereas canine/feline primary erythrocytosis is essentially a diagnosis of exclusion with only anecdotal JAK2 confirmation in dogs and none supported in cats, so the JAK-driven mechanism that anchors human PV drug biology is not established in pets.
source_name: Beurlet et al., Exp Hematol 2011; Merck Vet Manual (Erythrocytosis in Animals)
note: Merck does call it an uncommon MPN of dogs, cats and calves treated with phlebotomy/hydroxyurea, so the edge is defensible, but the Beurlet paper reports JAK2 V617F/C618L in only a single dog — molecular equivalence is not established.

vet_counterpart_name: Canine Pneumocystis pneumonia (Pneumocystis canis)
key_difference: Pneumocystis is strictly host-specific: dogs are infected by the genetically distinct P. canis and cannot be infected by human P. jirovecii, and the predisposing immunodeficiency is inherited (CARMIL2 nonsense variant / IgG deficiency in Cavalier King Charles Spaniels and miniature dachshunds) rather than the HIV/iatrogenic immunosuppression that dominates human PCP.
source_name: J Fungi 2024;10(3):198 (Coffey et al., CARMIL2 immunodeficiency in CKCS)
note: Same clinical entity type (interstitial pneumonia in an immunodeficient host) and authors call the dogs 'a valuable natural model', but different pathogen species and different host-immunity route; rare and breed-restricted in dogs, and I found no established feline PCP. I did not see a PMID rendered on the page so left it empty rather than guess.

vet_counterpart_name: Canine oral malignant melanoma (mucosal melanoma)
key_difference: Canine melanoma arises predominantly in sun-shielded oral mucosa and is consistently BRAF wild-type with no UV mutational signature, low point-mutation burden and copy-number/structural-variant-driven biology -- it maps onto human MUCOSAL melanoma, not the UV- and BRAF V600-driven cutaneous melanoma (~60% BRAF-mutant) that dominates the human 'Melanoma' concept and its approved drugs.
source_name: Nat Commun 2019;10:353 -- cross-species genomic comparison of human mucosal, canine oral and equine melanoma
note: High-impact bridge risk: BRAF/MEK inhibitors linked to the human melanoma node would transfer to a BRAF-wild-type canine disease where they are mechanistically inapplicable.

vet_counterpart_name: Cholangitis/cholangiohepatitis complex; feline hepatic lipidosis; canine vacuolar hepatopathy
key_difference: Intrahepatic cholestasis in dogs/cats is a secondary pathophysiologic state driven by infectious/inflammatory cholangitis, hepatic lipidosis or vacuolar hepatopathy, whereas MeSH D002780 in humans is anchored by primary autoimmune/genetic cholangiopathies (PBC, PSC, PFIC, intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy) for which I found no evidence of recognized canine or feline counterparts.
source_name: J Small Anim Pract 2022 (Jaffey, Canine intrahepatic biliary disease); WSAVA feline cholangitis classification
note: Edge is partly defensible because the shared cholestatic state is real and ursodiol is used in both species, but it is a syndrome not a disease -- sim=1.0 asserts identity with human primary cholangiopathies that do not occur in dogs/cats. Absence of PBC/PSC in dogs/cats is an absence-of-evidence finding from my searches, not a cited positive statement, so treat that half with mild caution.

vet_counterpart_name: Canine thyroid carcinoma (follicular/medullary); feline thyroid adenoma-hyperplasia
key_difference: Species diverge sharply in both directions: dogs get predominantly large, invasive, NON-functional follicular or medullary carcinomas (papillary rare, RAS/RAF signalling largely uninvolved, ~1/3 metastatic at diagnosis), cats get >95% BENIGN functional adenoma/hyperplasia causing hyperthyroidism, while human thyroid cancer is ~85% papillary, BRAF/RAS-driven and comparatively indolent.
source_name: Vet Pathol 2024 (Athey et al., canine thyroid carcinoma review); VSSO thyroid tumor monographs
note: One MeSH term is being forced to cover three materially different diseases across human, dog and cat; the dominant histology and driver pathways differ in each.

vet_counterpart_name: Copper-associated chronic hepatitis / copper toxicosis (Bedlington Terrier, Labrador Retriever, Doberman Pinscher)
key_difference: The canine disease is purely hepatic - the 'lenticular' half does not occur: 'Neurologic and ocular signs as a result of copper accumulation have not been observed in dogs with CAH', no Kayser-Fleischer rings - and the classic Bedlington model is driven by COMMD1 deletion rather than the human ATP7B.
source_name: Frontiers in Veterinary Science 2025 comparative review (DOI 10.3389/fvets.2025.1692603)
note: Real, well-studied comparative model (ATP7B variants do occur in Labradors/Dobermans, and COMMD1 stabilizes ATP7B, so mechanisms partly overlap), but the 2025 review explicitly treats canine CAH and human Wilson disease as related-but-distinct entities; a Wilson-model review (PMC6531654) independently states 'neurological features are absent as are Kayser-Fleischer rings' in Bedlingtons. Sim=1.0 overstates a liver-only phenotype as a hepatolenticular one.

vet_counterpart_name: Immune-mediated hemolytic anemia (IMHA)
source_name: ACVIM consensus statement 2019 (Garden et al., diagnosis of IMHA in dogs and cats)
note: Same core pathophysiology (anti-erythrocyte autoantibody, DAT/Coombs-positive, predominantly warm IgG extravascular hemolysis) and the ACVIM panel explicitly adapted human AIHA guidelines; caveat is that thromboembolism complicates 30-50% of canine cases, far above the human rate, so severity/natural history differ even though the entity does not.

source_name: Front Vet Sci 2022 comparative oncology review
note: Source states canine phenotypes are 'closely matched' to human OSA in pathology, progression, treatment options and prognosis; only clear divergence is age distribution (dogs 7-9 yr vs human pediatric peak), which does not change the entity — feline OSA exists but was not verified in this audit.

source_name: MSD/Merck Vet Manual, Feline Bronchial Asthma
note: Cats only - MSD describes Th2/IgE-driven eosinophilic inflammation, airway hyperresponsiveness, obstruction and remodeling as in people, and it is widely called a natural model of human allergic asthma; two caveats: true asthma is essentially absent in DOGS (canine counterpart is chronic bronchitis), and MSD notes cats are exceptionally serotonin-responsive, serotonin being the dominant allergen-induced bronchoconstrictor - a mediator difference that matters for drug repurposing.

source_name: 'Canine Primary Intracranial Cancer: A Clinicopathologic and Comparative Review of Glioma, Meningioma, and Choroid Plexus Tumors' (PMC6856054)
note: Dogs are the only non-human mammal that spontaneously develops brain tumours at human-like frequency (~3% at necropsy) with a parallel spectrum of histologic types; caveat at subtype level - canine gliomas lack the IDH1 R132H mutation found in ~70% of human low-grade gliomas and resemble human PEDIATRIC glioma, so 1.0 is fair for the umbrella term but would overstate an adult-glioma-specific claim.

source_name: 'Spontaneous dog osteoarthritis - a One Medicine vision', Nature Reviews Rheumatology 2019 (PMC7097182)
note: Source states the histopathology and pathogenesis of dog OA most closely resemble those of PRIMARY human OA, with shared joint sites (knee/hip/shoulder/elbow) and shared pain neurophysiology; only caveat is that canine OA more often follows joint instability (hip dysplasia, cranial cruciate rupture) - but 20-40% of human hip OA likewise traces to developmental dysplasia, so the etiologic gap is smaller than it first appears.

source_name: Transl Oncol 2022, Canine insulinoma as a model for human malignant insulinoma research
note: Canine insulinoma is genuinely an islet cell CARCINOMA (~95% malignant/metastatic) and the source validates it as a model with recurrence and prognosis similar to human malignant insulinoma; caveat worth flagging is that human insulinomas overall are ~90% benign, so only the malignant human subset matches, and MeSH D018273 also spans gastrinoma/glucagonoma which are rare in dogs.

source_name: J Vet Cardiol 2015 'The Genetic Basis of Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy in Cats and Humans' (PMID 26776594)
note: Feline familial HCM maps onto human familial HCM closely: same sarcomeric gene MYBPC3 (A31P in Maine Coon, R820W/R818W in Ragdoll), autosomal dominant with incomplete penetrance and variable expressivity in both. This is a cat disease - HCM is uncommon in dogs. Minor caveats: only two causal variants are confirmed in cats vs ~11 sarcomeric genes in humans, and most non-pedigree clinical feline HCM has no identified mutation, so the 'familial' label fits the breed cases best.

source_name: ICADA 2023 updated definition (Eisenschenk et al., Vet Dermatol 2024;35(1):3-4, doi:10.1111/vde.13183)
note: ICADA 2023 defines canine AD as a hereditary, pruritic, predominantly T-cell driven inflammatory skin disease with skin barrier abnormalities, allergen sensitization and microbial dysbiosis - the same core axis as human AD, and it is the best-regarded spontaneous model; residual doubt: lesion distribution differs and the feline entity is a looser umbrella ('feline atopic skin syndrome'), so 1.0 is defensible for dogs but slightly generous for cats. I did not obtain a PMID for the ICADA paper - DOI/citation seen in search results only.

source_name: MSD Vet Manual, 'Gammopathies in Animals'
note: Same clinical entity in dogs and cats -- plasma cell neoplasm with monoclonal gammopathy, lytic lesions of skull/ribs/pelvis/vertebrae, hyperviscosity, treated with chemotherapy (melphalan/prednisone); minor difference is that canine myeloma proteins are commonly IgA as well as IgG.

source_name: PLoS One 2012, 'Frequent alteration of the tumor suppressor gene APC in sporadic canine colorectal tumors'
note: Canine colorectal adenocarcinoma follows the same adenoma-carcinoma sequence with APC altered in ~70% of tumors and disrupted beta-catenin-binding repeats, and authors call sporadic canine CRCs valid molecular models of human CRC; caveat is that it is far rarer in dogs and no hereditary FAP/Lynch equivalent is described.

source_name: Merck Vet Manual
note: Identical causative organisms (C. neoformans, C. gattii) and identical drug classes (fluconazole/itraconazole, amphotericin B) across species; presentation skews nasal/upper-respiratory in cats and disseminated/CNS in >50% of dogs, and unlike humans it commonly affects apparently immunocompetent animals, but the entity is the same.

source_name: Merck Vet Manual; Vet Dermatol 2013 (cutaneous NTM granulomas in cats and dogs)
note: Same organisms as human NTM disease (M. avium complex, M. fortuitum, M. chelonae, M. kansasii, several explicitly zoonotic) and same drug classes; caveat is that pet disease is dominated by cutaneous/subcutaneous granulomas and disseminated MAC rather than the pulmonary MAC that dominates human NTM, and feline leprosy agents (M. lepraemurium) have no direct human counterpart.

source_name: Merck Vet Manual
note: Same core pathophysiology and same subtype split as humans (80-85% ACTH-secreting pituitary adenoma, 10-15% adrenal tumor); most common in dogs but cats are affected; differences are quantitative or peripheral (canine incidence ~1000x human, ectopic ACTH not reported in dogs, USP8 mutations absent from canine corticotroph adenomas) and do not change the entity.

source_name: Vet Comp Oncol / PubMed 34296746 (canine+feline vs human angiosarcoma oncogenomics)
note: Same malignant endothelial tumor in dogs and cats; recurrent drivers TP53, PIK3CA, ATRX, GRIN2A, LRP1B shared with human angiosarcoma, and AACR/MCR comparative genomics calls canine HSA a close model of human visceral/breast angiosarcoma. Caveat: anatomic distribution differs (canine HSA mostly splenic/right-atrial and common; human AS mostly cutaneous/breast, rare, often radiation- or vinyl-chloride-associated) but this is epidemiologic, not a different entity.

source_name: Vet Clin Pathol 1991, 'Proposed criteria for classification of acute myeloid leukemia in dogs and cats' (human FAB/NCI criteria adapted)
note: Same entity in dogs and cats and Cornell states canine AML mimics the human counterpart clinically, but canonical human drivers NPM1/DNMT3A appear absent in dogs and feline AML is often erythroid/MDS-associated, so driver-targeted human drugs may not transfer.

source_name: MSD Vet Manual
note: MSD: 'recognized primarily in dogs'; same PTH-deficiency entity with the same causes as humans (idiopathic lymphocytic/immune-mediated parathyroiditis, post-surgical) and same calcium/vitamin D therapy; the page does not cover cats, so feline occurrence is unverified here.

source_name: MSD/Merck Vet Manual (Diabetes Insipidus in Animals); JAVMA 1996 20-case series
note: Same core pathophysiology (deficient ADH secretion from hypothalamic/pars nervosa axis), same modified water-deprivation diagnostic logic, same desmopressin treatment; MSD explicitly separates central from nephrogenic form as in humans. Uncommon but genuine in both dogs and cats.

source_name: MSD/Merck Vet Manual (Histoplasmosis in Animals)
note: MSD defines it directly as 'a chronic, noncontagious, disseminated, granulomatous disease of humans and other animals due to the dimorphic fungus Histoplasma capsulatum' -- identical organism and mechanism; only organ tropism shifts (GI/hepatic predominant in dogs, respiratory/cutaneous in cats), which I judged insufficient to downgrade.

source_name: LeishVet guidelines (canine leishmaniosis directions)
note: Same parasite species (Leishmania infantum), same sandfly vector, same zoonotic cycle with the dog as the main urban reservoir; a well-established veterinary disease with its own consensus guidelines. Caveat I could not verify head-to-head: canine disease is characteristically viscerocutaneous whereas human L. infantum infection presents as visceral leishmaniasis, and MeSH D007896 is a broad parent term spanning cutaneous/visceral/mucocutaneous forms -- so the edge is sound but the term is coarse. Feline leishmaniosis exists but I did not research it, hence occurs_in 'dog'.

source_name: MSD/Merck Vet Manual (Cushing Syndrome/Hyperadrenocorticism in Animals)
note: Same cell of origin and same functional adenoma/adenocarcinoma spectrum causing cortisol excess (adrenal-dependent hyperadrenocorticism = 10-15% of canine cases), with mitotane -- also the human adrenocortical carcinoma drug -- effective in both. Weaker points I flag honestly: canine tumors are usually detected because they are functional whereas human adrenocortical neoplasms are mostly non-functional incidentalomas, canine carcinoma prognosis is far better than human ACC, and MSD gave only limited detail on cats (rare; feline tumors more often aldosterone-secreting).
